data.title: Starboard blasts Tripadvisor for slow AI adoption, calls for board change
data.description:
Activist investor Starboard Value LP said it will nominate a slate of directors that will represent a majority of Tripadvisor's (TRIP) board at the upcoming annual meeting and called for the exploration of strategic alternatives, including a sale of the company.

Shares of Tripadvisor (TRIP) are up more than 11% in afternoon trading on Tuesday.

In its letter, Starboard expressed its frustration and lack of confidence in Tripadvisor's top brass and its board. 

Starboard said the company failed to act fast to implement changes that would unlock maximum value for shareholders, and under CEO Matt Goldberg, the stock lost nearly half its value.

In particular, the investor complained about the slow adoption of AI at the company while being a top-cited website by AI models.

"Tripadvisor has been massively late and incredibly slow to develop and launch a compelling AI-enabled version of Tripadvisor," Starboard said in the letter. "In a period of such rapid innovation and change, and with such an advantaged position, Tripadvisor appears to be squandering its lead."

"We believe there remains a meaningful value creation opportunity at the company, but we do not have confidence that this management team and board can deliver it. We believe Tripadvisor should be operated and governed by those with the sense of urgency, the resources, the skillsets, and the incentives to move quickly and execute at the level required to win in a rapidly changing world. The time for incrementalism is over," Starboard concluded.

data.title: Liberty Energy raised to Buy at BofA as surging U.S. power demand means long growth runway
data.description:
Liberty Energy (LBRT) +2.6% in Tuesday's trading as Bank of America upgraded shares to Buy from Neutral with a $31 price target, raised from $20, seeing the company as a best-in-class pressure pumper with advantaged technology, including in electric fracking, strong vertical integration, and deep customer relationships.

After two years of cyclical decline, BofA analysts led by Saurabh Pant believe activity and pricing in Liberty's (LBRT) completions business will bottom in 2026 and should be followed by a recovery in 2027-28, and they see upside from the company's structural growth opportunity in its behind-the-meter power business as it ramps up to its targeted 3 GW deployed capacity by year-end 2029.

With long lead times for traditional utility expansion and four-year-long interconnection queues plus growing regulatory headwinds, data centers are turning to behind-the-meter power in a big way, which allows Liberty (LBRT) to leverage its oilfield expertise, including in electric completions, to grow in BTM power, Pant said.

The analyst expects starting and significantly growing a new BTM power business will come with its fair share of execution challenges, but Liberty's (LBRT) track record of technology leadership and methodical business development, plus recent contracts with Vantage and an as-yet unnamed data center developer in Texas, and recent $700M convertibles deal help advance the company's power ambition.

data.title: Knife River targets $3.3B–$3.5B revenue and $520M–$560M adjusted EBITDA for 2026 while advancing vertical integration strategy
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Knife River Corporation (KNF) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Brian Gray stated that 2025 was "a year of meaningful strategic progress," emphasizing the achievement of a 7% adjusted EBITDA growth to $497 million, the completion of five acquisitions, and continued investment in EDGE initiatives. Gray reported, "We entered 2026 with confidence and clear sense of momentum. We believe we are well positioned to continue growing our business."
  • Gray highlighted Knife River's four-pronged growth strategy: strong markets, vertical integration, margin improvement through self-help, and a performance-driven company culture. He said Knife River's backlog reached a record $1 billion, up 38% from the prior year, with about 90% public work.
  • Gray noted, "2025 was an active year for acquisitions, and we expect an equally busy year in 2026. We've completed 1 bolt-on deal already in Montana and expect they will be announcing more deals before the busy construction season starts."
  • In the West segment, Gray cited "record profitability in our Legacy Pacific operations more than offset a softer economy in Oregon" and pointed to strong public activity in California, Alaska, and Hawaii, including the $3 billion Navy dry dock project in Hawaii. Oregon's financial results improved, and Gray expects "Oregon's performance in 2026 to be broadly in line with 2025 results."
  • The Mountain segment saw "construction revenue... up almost 20% for the quarter." Gray said the region will benefit from "record backlog entering 2026 which contains more asphalt paving than we performed in all of 2025."
  • Central segment growth was driven by three acquisitions and integration of Strata, the largest in Knife River's history. Gray expects "meaningful volume growth from the addition of Texcrete, our new ready-mix operations in Central Texas."
  • CFO Nathan Ring reported, "we finished 2025 with an impressive fourth quarter that produced 47% higher adjusted EBITDA and a 340 basis point improvement in adjusted EBITDA margin. Across our product lines, gross profit was up 27% for the quarter, and we achieved a record gross margin of nearly 19%."

Outlook

  • For 2026, Knife River expects consolidated revenue between $3.3 billion and $3.5 billion and adjusted EBITDA between $520 million and $560 million, implying an adjusted EBITDA margin of approximately 16% at the midpoint. Ring stated, "As usual, this guidance assumes normal weather, economic and operating conditions."
  • Management expects aggregates volumes to grow mid-single digits, pricing to increase mid-single digits, and continued aggregates margin expansion of approximately 200 basis points in 2026. Ready-mix volumes are projected to improve in the mid-teens.
  • SG&A is expected to be in line with 2025 as a percentage of revenue. Maintenance and improvement capital expenditures are projected to remain between 5% and 7% of revenue, with additional $131 million for organic growth projects and reserve additions.
  • No guidance comparison to analysts' estimates, as data was not provided.

Financial Results

  • The fourth quarter delivered 47% higher adjusted EBITDA and a 340 basis point improvement in adjusted EBITDA margin, per Ring. Gross profit rose 27% for the quarter, with gross margin reaching nearly 19%.
  • Aggregates volumes increased 17% and pricing rose 8%, aided by acquisitions. Aggregates gross margins increased by 200 basis points. Ready-mix volumes grew 20% with a 230 basis point gross margin lift. Asphalt internal sales volumes rose more than 8%. Contracting services revenue was up 15%, though gross margin declined due to backlog mix and project timing.
  • Backlog at year-end was $1 billion, a 38% increase year-over-year, with 75% expected to be completed in 2026.
  • Unrestricted cash was nearly $75 million, with $475 million available on the revolving credit facility and net leverage of 2.2x.

Q&A

  • Brent Thielman, D.A. Davidson: Asked about West segment backlog and margin opportunities. Gray responded that while backlog shifted geographically to Mountain and Central, "very solid funding in California, Hawaii and Alaska" remains, and Oregon's DOT budget is "about flat, slightly up."
  • Thielman: Queried on aggregate pricing levers. Ring replied, "very strong year... for aggregate pricing, high single digits," with mid-single-digit increases expected in 2026, partly due to the Strata acquisition. Gray added that dynamic pricing tools are now fully implemented at legacy sites.
  • Ethan Roberts, Stephens Inc.: Asked about margin outlook. Gray explained that margin improvement is broad-based, but the shift toward Mountain and Central regions, which have lower margins than West, moderates consolidated margin growth.
  • Roberts: Inquired on Oregon growth triggers. Gray stated, "I feel comfortable saying that the results for Oregon in 2026 should be in line with what we had in 2025," noting ongoing legislative discussions on infrastructure funding.
  • Kathryn Thompson, Thompson Research Group: Asked about self-help vs. pricing in margin gains. Gray credited favorable weather, acquisition contributions, and operational execution, highlighting EDGE initiatives and cost controls.
  • Thompson: Asked about M&A and organic pipeline. Gray described a "robust" pipeline, favoring aggregates-based, vertically integrated bolt-ons, with disciplined due diligence. Ring assured liquidity and balance sheet flexibility for further acquisitions.
  • Garik Shmois, Loop Capital: Queried on SG&A trends. Ring cited acquisition-related costs as the largest driver but expects SG&A as a percent of revenue to be stable.
  • Shmois: Asked about volume strength versus peers. Gray pointed to new capacity from Texcrete and backlog-driven aggregates and ready-mix growth.
  • Ian Zaffino, Oppenheimer: Sought details on data center work. Gray emphasized the upside, noting "virtually 0 amount of dollars in our backlog related to data centers" but significant pending bids and higher-margin material supply.
  • Garrett Greenblatt, JPMorgan: Asked about acquisition impact on 2026 outlook. Gray said, "really, you could look at all of that growth as being organic at this point in time," with guidance not including future acquisitions.
  • Ivan Yi, Wolfe Research: Requested color on 1Q 2026 seasonality. Ring referenced typical seasonal loss patterns, with some offset from Texcrete acquisition.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on backlog composition, margin outlook, and regional performance, displaying a generally positive but probing tone. Questions reflected confidence in management's operational execution while seeking clarity on margin drivers and M&A plans.
  • Management maintained a confident and constructive tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, frequently referencing "momentum," "record backlog," and "well positioned for growth." Gray and Ring provided detailed responses and did not appear defensive or evasive. Phrases like "we are well positioned" and "we have built good momentum" underscored their confidence.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analysts displayed increased optimism, with more emphasis on operational progress and the company's strengthened growth platform.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance increased, with 2026 revenue projected between $3.3 billion and $3.5 billion and adjusted EBITDA between $520 million and $560 million, compared to the prior quarter's 2025 guidance of $3.1 billion–$3.15 billion revenue and $475 million–$500 million adjusted EBITDA.
  • Management deepened its focus on vertical integration, dynamic pricing, and cost controls, reporting greater traction from these initiatives than in the previous quarter.
  • Analysts shifted focus from stabilization in Oregon and weather challenges to growth opportunities, M&A integration, and margin expansion.
  • The tone from both management and analysts was more confident, reflecting the company's operational progress, record backlog, and successful acquisitions.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing legislative discussions on infrastructure funding in Oregon and noted a "fluid" DOT funding landscape. Gray said, "clarity on that will be important," but expressed comfort with a stable outlook.
  • SG&A increases were attributed to acquisitions, but Ring expects stabilization as synergies are realized.
  • Margin improvement is moderated by a mix shift away from higher-margin West segment toward Mountain and Central regions.
  • Data center-related work, while promising, is not yet material to backlog or guidance.

Final Takeaway

Knife River Corporation closed 2025 with record profitability, a robust acquisition pipeline, and substantial momentum in vertical integration and cost control initiatives. Management targets continued growth in 2026, projecting consolidated revenue of $3.3–$3.5 billion and adjusted EBITDA of $520–$560 million. With a record $1 billion backlog, disciplined capital strategy, and a focus on expanding in high-growth markets, the company asserts it is well positioned to deliver further shareholder value in the coming year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Enbridge downgraded at Jefferies after Canadian midstream's YTD rally
data.description:
Enbridge (ENB) -3.3% in Tuesday's trading as Jefferies downgraded the Canadian pipeline company to Hold from Buy with a C$76 price target, saying the stock's recent re-rating now
adequately prices growth.

Enbridge (ENB) shares rallied to all-time highs Friday, likely driven more by constructive Venezuela commentary than by its Q4 earnings beat, Jefferies analyst Sam Burwell said, and the company touted data center upside, expecting to issue final investment decisions for more gas projects later this year.

The company maintained FY 2026 and long-term growth guidance, however, and Burwell has Enbridge (ENB) trading at 12.8x and 12.2x on 2027 and 2028 EBITDA, respectively, with shares up 12% YTD and re-rated by nearly a turn of EBITDA in the last three weeks to the highest multiple since 2022.

Enbridge (ENB) now trades at a ~0.5x discount to TC Energy (TRP), which Burwell believes is fair given the latter's greater growth visibility.

data.title: Apple makes AI push with trio of new and updated devices: report
data.description:
Apple (AAPL) appears to be probing deeper into the realm of artificial intelligence with new and updated products centered around the technology.

The Cupertino company, which has been criticized by some for not taking fuller advantage of AI, is developing new AI-powered smart glasses and a pendant while updating its popular AirPods, according to Bloomberg.   

Apple shares were up more than 3% during afternoon trading on Tuesday.

Its smart glasses will come equipped with a high-resolution camera, speakers, and a microphone, the report said. Similar to AirPods and the new pendant, it will rely on a nearby iPhone for the bulk of the computing power, even though it will feature a chip. Apple is most focused on the camera and design quality in the glasses, which have been code-named N50 within the company.

Apple reportedly plans to design its own frames rather than partner with an established eyeglass maker, such as Meta Platforms (META) with EssilorLuxottica (ESLOF) or Google (GOOG)(GOOGL) with Warby Parker (WRBY).

Meanwhile, the pendant will feature a camera and microphone and can be worn as a clip-on pin or on a necklace, the report said. It can serve as an always-on "eyes and ears" of the iPhone. It is expected to be about the same size as an Apple AirTag.

Finally, the updated AirPods are expected to contain more AI-powered features and could be released as soon as this year, the report said.

Earlier on Tuesday, Wedbush declared that 2026 is the year Apple will firmly establish itself as a dominant player in the age of AI.

"The elephant in the room remains the invisible AI strategy, with the biggest consumer installed base in the world of 2.5 billion iOS devices and 1.5 billion iPhones; the time is now for Apple to accelerate its AI efforts," said Wedbush analyst Dan Ives. "We believe the AI monetization piece could add $75 to $100 per share to the Apple story over the coming few years as it finally plays out in 2026."

data.title: Harmony Bio granted FDA label expansion for Wakix in children with narcolepsy
data.description:
• Harmony Biosciences (HRMY) announced on Tuesday that the U.S. FDA approved a label expansion for its sleep disorder therapy, Wakix, allowing its use in children with narcolepsy, a condition characterized by extreme daytime sleepiness.
  • Specifically, the FDA has approved Harmony Bio’s (HRMY) supplemental new drug application ((sNDA)) targeting the U.S. approval of Wakix in children aged six years and older with cataplexy, a sudden muscular weakness associated with narcolepsy.
  • The agency first approved Wakix (pitolisant) tablets in 2019 as a treatment for excessive daytime sleepiness in adults with narcolepsy and expanded its labeling in 2020 to include adults with cataplexy.
  • “Our sights are now set on further growing, extending, and expanding the value of pitolisant through additional indications with our next-generation formulations,” CEO Jeffrey Dayno remarked.

data.title: Volatility watch: Wingstop is setting up for a big earnings move
data.description:
Wingstop (WING) ripped a double-digit gain in afternoon trading on Tuesday as the restaurant operator's fourth-quarter earnings reports approach.

The company has a strong track record of topping earnings expectations. Over the last two years, WingStop (WING) has beaten EPS estimates 88% of the time and has beaten revenue estimates 100% of the time.

The conference call update could add more color to Wingstop's (WING) forecast for mid-teens global net unit growth in 2026. Analysts have noted that Wingstop's development pipeline remains at record levels, with strong demand from existing franchisees, reinforcing the asset-light model and positioning the brand to keep compounding system sales despite softer U.S. comparable sales marks. Management could also weigh in on Wingstop's (WING) international growth plans and the planned debuts of the brand in markets such as Australia, the Middle East, new parts of Europe, India, and other high-growth regions.

Shares of Wingstop (WING) showed a 12.8% gain at 2:33 p.m. on volume of over 1.1M. Short interest on WING stands at 13.2% of the total float. Options trading implies a share price swing of 14% after the earnings report drops. Wingstop (WING) is lined up to report earnings before the opening bell on Wednesday.

data.title: Anthropic continued to push model boundaries with latest Claude Sonnet 4.6 unveiling
data.description:
Fresh off its $30B funding round that valued it at $380B, Anthropic (ANTHRO) unveiled its Claude Sonnet 4.6 artificial intelligence model on Tuesday, its second release in two weeks.

In a statement on its website, Anthropic said Claude Sonnet 4.6 has several improvements in various areas, such as coding, long-context reasoning, agent planning, knowledge work, and design. It also has a 1M token context window in beta, the company added.

"Sonnet 4.6 brings much-improved coding skills to more of our users," Anthropic, which is backed by Amazon (AMZN), Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) and others, said. "Improvements in consistency, instruction following, and more have made developers with early access prefer Sonnet 4.6 to its predecessor by a wide margin. They often even prefer it to our smartest model from November 2025, Claude Opus 4.5."

"Performance that would have previously required reaching for an Opus-class model—including on real-world, economically valuable office tasks—is now available with Sonnet 4.6," Anthropic added. "The model also shows a major improvement in computer use skills compared to prior Sonnet models."

Anthropic added that Claude Sonnet 4.6 is available now on all Claude plans, Claude Cowork, Claude Code, the company's API, and all major cloud platforms. It can also be used by default on its free Claude tier, which has file creation, connectors, skills, and compaction.

Claude Opus 4.6 was released by Anthropic earlier this month.

data.title: Uber and Life360 to allow for greater platform integration
data.description:
• To make it easier for families to coordinate ridesharing and track loved ones in real-time, Uber (UBER) is expanding its partnership with Life360 (LIF) by enabling greater integration between platforms. 
  • Over the coming months, Uber (UBER) and Life360 (LIF) will deliver a more comprehensive app that can track trips in real time, allow for ride booking, and enable Life360 members to link with their Uber (UBER) accounts and vice versa.
  • “By linking Uber accounts with Life360, we’re creating a one-stop experience where families can stay informed when loved ones are on the move—especially parents of teens as they coordinate rides and track trips to school, activities, and more,” said Margarita Peker, Uber’s global head of Family Growth.
  • The Uber/Life360 integration will be available later this year.

data.title: USA Compression forecasts $770M–$800M adjusted EBITDA in 2026 while integrating J-W Power acquisition
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: USA Compression Partners, LP (USAC) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Micah Green, President & CEO, stated the company closed the J-W Power acquisition on January 12, expanding USA Compression’s reach across all major U.S. oil and gas basins and horsepower classes. He emphasized, “With this transaction, we are leaning into the USA Compression name with broader reach all across this great country.”
  • Green reported high operational performance, noting a “TRIR of 0.39, which is approximately half of the industry average,” and highlighted record results: “We delivered full year adjusted EBITDA of $613.8 million and DCF of $385.7 million, both are records for the company.”
  • The CEO pointed to strong utilization: “We maintained high average utilization in excess of 94% throughout the year and ended the year at 94.5%.”
  • Green described refinancing activities that reduced borrowing costs and increased strategic flexibility, and noted the company’s resilience through leadership changes, a new headquarters, a shared services model, and a new ERP platform.
  • Christopher Wauson, Senior VP & COO, stated, “Since the close of the transaction on January 12, we have begun planning to optimize route management, inventory, contracts and operational structures to begin to realize synergies as early as this year,” and outlined anticipated $10 million to $20 million in annual run rate synergies by the end of 2027.
  • Wauson reported, “We have already begun moving contracts under USA Compression MSAs, and we'll work to extend average contract duration throughout this year.”
  • The COO addressed the addition of approximately 200,000 idle horsepower from J-W, estimating about 50,000 horsepower is readily deployable, with the remainder under review for potential monetization.
  • Christopher Paulsen, Senior VP, CFO & Treasurer, stated, “In Q4, we increased pricing to an all-time high, averaging $21.69 per horsepower, a 1% increase in sequential quarters and a 4% increase compared to the year ago period.”
  • Paulsen outlined, “Our fourth quarter adjusted gross margins came in at 66.8%, right on historical trend.”

Outlook

  • Paulsen provided guidance for 2026: “We are forecasting adjusted EBITDA of $770 million to $800 million and distributable cash flow of $480 million to $510 million.”
  • Maintenance capital is forecasted at $60 million to $70 million, with expansion capital ranging from $230 million to $250 million, including just over 100,000 new horsepower and upgrades for improved telemetry practices.
  • Paulsen highlighted, “The net result of our budget should enable us to improve upon our debt metrics with our near-term targets at 3.75x debt-to-EBITDA, a quarter-turn improvement over the next 12 months.”
  • Management expects synergies from the J-W acquisition to be realized by 2027 and plans for “modest onetime cost associated with the transaction in 2026.”

Financial Results

  • Paulsen reported, “Our fourth quarter 2025 net income was $27.8 million, operating income was $76.6 million, net cash provided by operating activities was $139.5 million and cash interest expense net was $43.4 million.”
  • He stated, “Our total fleet horsepower at the end of the quarter was approximately 3.9 million horsepower, adding approximately 21,000 horsepower as compared to the prior quarter.”
  • Expansion capital expenditures for Q4 were $40 million and maintenance capital was $7.8 million. For the full year, adjusted EBITDA reached $613.8 million, and distributable cash flow was $385.7 million, “above the recently increased guidance, in part due to the final preferred unit conversion in December.”

Q&A

  • Douglas Irwin, Citigroup: Asked for a breakdown of the $250 million growth budget and its run rate moving forward, as well as the impact of long lead times. Paulsen responded, “About $205 million of growth capital is tied in with the typical compression business, both new units, make ready and reconfigurations...approximately $150 million of that $205 million is tied to new units.” He noted long lead times, especially for larger units, but cited the flexibility provided by the J-W manufacturing business.
  • Irwin: Asked about balance sheet improvements and potential for distribution growth. Paulsen replied, “Our normalized number is about 1.55x on Q4. We're looking for that number to be in the 1.6x plus range next year or this coming year that is. And as that number starts to expand beyond 1.6x...we need to continue to have conversations with all of our unitholders as to what the right answer is in terms of distribution growth.”
  • James Rollyson, Raymond James: Inquired about the timing of the 105,000 capacity adds. Green stated, “I think most of what's coming on this year is in the back half of the year, July forward.” Wauson added, “The bulk of the horsepower comes in late Q3 into Q4.”
  • Rollyson: Asked about lead times and equipment cost trends. Green replied, “The main driver in the lead times is Caterpillar engines and the data center demand for generation has driven that lead time out...I expect we'll see some type of increase at some point this year. I haven't heard of one yet. But I'm sure 1 will come down later on this year.”
  • Gabriel Moreen, Mizuho: Asked about the distributed power space. Green indicated they have evaluated opportunities but have not pursued any yet, continuing to monitor for suitable models.
  • Moreen: Sought clarification on placing the remaining 50% of new HP for 2026. Wauson expressed confidence in contracting the balance soon.
  • Nate Pendleton, Texas Capital Bank: Asked about long-term horsepower growth strategy amid long lead times. Wauson said, “It's not going to affect our 2026 growth, but in 2027, we are working to secure that and figure that out,” with the J-W manufacturing business providing optionality.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed for details on capital allocation, timing, lead times, pricing trends, and distribution policy, reflecting a neutral to slightly positive tone as they sought clarity and confirmation of growth plans.
  • Management maintained a confident and constructive tone, repeatedly referencing operational strength, integration benefits, and future prospects. Phrases such as “We are confident” and “We look forward to creating unitholder value” indicated optimism. During Q&A, management acknowledged challenges but emphasized flexibility and proactive planning.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone shifted toward greater strategic ambition with the J-W acquisition and a more explicit focus on integration synergies and expanded manufacturing capabilities. Analysts’ tone remained consistent, focused on growth visibility and capital efficiency.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 is more detailed, with specific adjusted EBITDA and DCF targets, compared to the previous quarter’s preliminary outlook.
  • Strategic focus has shifted to integration of J-W Power, with explicit synergy targets and expanded manufacturing capabilities, contrasting with the prior quarter’s emphasis on organic growth and cost savings.
  • Analysts in both quarters focused on capacity additions, pricing, and capital deployment, but the latest call included more questions about manufacturing lead times and strategic flexibility.
  • Management’s confidence level increased, emphasizing new opportunities from the acquisition and flexibility in addressing supply chain challenges.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited increased lead times for new equipment—now over two years—as a significant challenge, with plans to mitigate through the J-W manufacturing business and internal planning.
  • The integration of 200,000 idle horsepower from J-W poses utilization and monetization risks, with management stating, “We will analyze the best path, including potential monetization of a portion of the horsepower.”
  • Expansion capital plans and onetime costs associated with the acquisition could affect short-term margins, though management expects margin alignment and synergy realization by 2027.

Final Takeaway

USA Compression Partners closed the transformative J-W Power acquisition, setting ambitious targets for 2026 with forecasted adjusted EBITDA of $770 million to $800 million and a focus on integration synergies, expanded manufacturing capabilities, and operational flexibility. Management emphasized confidence in maintaining strong utilization, improving debt metrics, and capturing new growth opportunities across key U.S. basins, while actively managing risks related to lead times and asset deployment as they pursue sustained unitholder value.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
